data.title: IREN: 88% Upside Potential As AI Revenue Scales Toward $3.4 Billion ARR
data.description:
Investment Thesis

IREN Limited (IREN) is one of the new players in the field of cloud infrastructure for AI, offering a unique advantage in the form of existing capacity required for the deployment of data centers. Earlier, this company was valued by the market as a "proxy for Bitcoin," carrying out large-scale mining of the first cryptocurrency. But in 2026, it’s expected that IREN will fully transition to being an infrastructure player in the AI computing space, backed up by a long-term contract signed with Microsoft (MSFT). Considering investors' interest in IREN shares as a new player in the AI field, the key to maintaining the previous growth in capitalization will be achieving the Annualized Run-Rate Revenue (ARR) targets in the AI Cloud segment. IREN management expects results in the billions of dollars by the end of 2026. I would like to take a closer look at the company's growth catalysts, how it competes with other companies, as well as the risks that may arise during its transformation. Given the results, it is my opinion that IREN shares still have significant growth potential, so my recommendation for this asset is "Buy."

IREN's Strategy and Growth Drivers

From 2024, IREN Limited has been undergoing an active transformation from a conventional Bitcoin miner to an infrastructure player in the field of AI computing. Now, the company is using its capacity, formerly used for blockchain network calculations, for next-gen data center operations. Their rebranding and reorganization by the end of 2024 show that the company has picked a new growth strategy, using current trends in AI development. Their main goal is to transition to AI Cloud dominance in the company's EBITDA structure. Still, as of early 2026, we think IREN should keep splitting into two main business areas because Bitcoin mining is still the main source of EBITDA, based on data for Q1 FY2026.

As an example, if we break down IREN's revenue, the Bitcoin mining part is $232.9 million. It managed to mine 2,039 Bitcoin coins with an electricity cost of $0.046 per kWh. Meanwhile, its AI Cloud service only brought in $7.3 million, showing how small this new business is still in the overall financial results. However, by 2026, the balance of these revenue sources is expected to change, and the target margin for AI Cloud is set at around 85%.

The decision to maintain the capacity to mine Bitcoin is a sound one, since transitioning to an infrastructure player in AI requires time, during which not only new-generation data centers are deployed, but also work is carried out to establish contractual relationships with customers who will be provided with the computing power itself. Parallel to the operation of its own data centers, IREN is deploying ASIC miners. The company's competitive advantages, which include ownership of the land where its energy infrastructure facilities are located, also contribute to this diversification of its business. Another point to consider is that IREN follows a green agenda, utilizing 100% renewable energy to power its data centers. The energy is mainly generated by hydroelectric power plants and wind turbines located in British Columbia (Canada) and Texas (USA).

Nevertheless, the fourfold increase in IREN's market capitalization in 2025 would not have been possible if it had not developed a new business line in the field of AI. The company is utilizing its existing mining infrastructure to convert it into next-generation data centers. As a result, it is able to provide cloud services for AI computing. What I mean by "new generation" is IREN's data centers, using special liquid cooling (Horizon), enabling more efficient cooling of operating graphics processors, thereby increasing their operating power without the risk of overheating the equipment itself. As the main driver of IREN's stock growth in 2026, I would point to the $9.7 billion contract signed with Microsoft. Partner agreements between the companies are organized for 5 years, throughout which IREN is obliged to provide the necessary computing power based on NVIDIA (NVDA) GPUs such as H100/H200 and Blackwell in order to support the training and operation of MSFT neural networks.

Competition for IREN

Analyzing IREN's competitive environment, I would also highlight competitors in two main business areas: the Bitcoin mining sector and the AI cloud infrastructure sector. When it comes to mining, however, there are direct competitors that, like IREN, use hybrid AI miners. That is, they are the same companies that are converting their facilities into data centers. In this market segment, there are 4 main players. Core Scientific (CORZ) is the most serious competitor. Although it previously went bankrupt, CORZ managed to get out of its financial hole by reorganizing its business and has already received a $9 billion takeover bid from CoreWeave (CRWV). Nevertheless, major shareholders of the company did not approve this transaction. A competitive advantage of CORZ is that the company has enormous amounts of available electricity and currently leases 400 MW of data processing capacity to new customers.

Its competitors also include companies such as Hut 8 (HUT), undergoing a similar transformation to IREN, though at a slower pace. Also worth mentioning is TeraWulf (WULF), the competitive advantage of which is its image as an environmentally friendly company that maintains AI capacity through the use of nuclear energy. Among other competitors, one can mention Applied Digital (APLD), who were the first to begin the transformation from a traditional miner to an AI player, extending their current order portfolio to $16 billion.

There are also several companies that have been traditional Bitcoin miners to date. They are MARA Holdings (MARA), Riot Platforms (RIOT), and CleanSpark (CLSK). These companies are betting on hashrate, affordable energy access, or increased cryptocurrency mining. Management at IREN has chosen a different path of development, so it is more relevant to analyze competition among companies that are specialized in AI clouds. I believe that the main competitor here is Nebius (NBIS), and in a recent article about them, they discussed the reasons why their shares are recommended as a "Buy." Literally just recently, in September 2025, Nebius signed a $17.4 billion agreement with Microsoft to provide the computing power needed to run the Azure cloud application. The company was able to do this thanks to its data centers around the world. Its data center in Finland, for example, has been recognized as one of the most energy-efficient in the world. NBIS also has data center facilities in the US, Israel, and France.

But, apart from NBIS, IREN's competitors are CoreWeave and Lambda Labs. Just like NBIS, CoreWeave has the same competitive advantages, including priority access to the latest and best NVIDIA chips. In addition, the CRWV is capable of attracting large credit lines secured by the GPUs themselves. Meanwhile, Lambda is recognized as one of the most established players in the cloud infrastructure market, offering NVIDIA chips for rent and benefiting from deep integration into the developer ecosystem.

IREN's Financial Profile and Expectations for 2026

The financial profile of IREN's business is still far from ideal (despite its enormous potential for future profits), with all the Valuation Grade group's metrics still showing inflated values. But this is normal for a company that's aggressively scaling up as it enters a new, quickly growing market. In addition, based on data for Q1 FY2026, the company's revenue was $240.3 million, a 348.61% increase compared to the same period in FY2025. The figures were $11.8 million higher than the consensus forecast. Speaking of Non-GAAP EPS, it turned out to be $1.08 instead of the expected $0.14.

But for 2026, things are looking way more promising, if you ask me. Firstly, they're expecting to see the first results from the contract they signed with Microsoft. Management estimates that once everything's up and running, they'll be pulling in about $1.9 billion a year. Furthermore, they plan to significantly boost their revenue in the AI cloud segment, with a record $3.4 billion expected in 2026. For this, data center capacity is expected to keep growing, and GPUs will go from 23,000 to 140,000 units. With this in mind, back in October 2025, they brought in an extra $875 million by issuing interest-free convertible bonds (and an extra $125 million through an option for an additional buyback), which bumped up their liquidity balance to $1.8 billion. It then conducted another issue of convertible bonds in December 2025 for $2 billion with an additional option for $300 million. This money will be used not only to purchase new GPUs but also to implement its project portfolio. In April 2026, it plans to launch the Sweetwater 1 substation with a capacity of 1.4 GW. At the end of 2027, it will launch a second phase with an additional 600 MW.

Awaiting Q2 FY26 Quarterly Results

The IREN consensus forecast indicates an expected negative EPS of -0.09 dollars and revenue of 227.3 million dollars. Consensus forecasts indicate an expected negative EPS of -$0.09 and revenue of $227.33 million. Over the past three months, however, forecasts for EPS and revenue have been revised nine times, each time downward. I believe that the probability of seeing better-than-expected data is quite high, given that the last two quarterly reports came in with positive surprises. Furthermore, analysts' current consensus forecast includes concerns discussed in December 2025 about a possible slowdown in the AI supercycle. Considering the signed MSFT contract and capacity scaling, I expect IREN's actual results to be much better than initially expected by the consensus forecast of analysts.

IREN Valuation

In order to assess IREN's potential value, we must take into account that using traditional P/E multiples is not reasonable, since the company has an enormous CAPEX. As a result, other multiples should be used. An example is the EV/Sales valuation. The company's revenue forecast for 2026 is currently $3.4 billion. The EV/Sales ratio would therefore be 5.58x. The EV/Sales ratios of its main competitors are higher. NBIS, for example, based on a projected revenue of $3.45 billion for 2026, would have a multiplier of 7.86x. The market estimates CRWV at 13.14x. Taking their median, we get 10.5x. This means that if we revalue the shares to the median EV/Sales multiplier, the IREN price will go up by 88%, going from the current $55.30 to $103.96.

IREN Risks

To my mind, a big risk for IREN stock is tied to how it's changing its business. Despite signing a deal with MSFT, a few critical things still need to be considered. This is especially true if it leads to a mismatch between how fast costs and revenue are growing. The year 2026 will provide market participants with answers to questions about the profitability of the AI Cloud segment. The IREN management has set a target of 85%, an extremely high estimate, yet realistic given the technological expertise of the company's team. First and foremost, this problem of mismatch is possible due to potential delays in deploying additional infrastructure. This is because the company is planning a huge increase in capacity from 23,000 GPUs to 140,000 GPUs by the end of 2026. And any delays, such as connecting to the ERCOT grid, could push back when the new capacity starts generating revenue.

Unfortunately, we also note that IREN is becoming highly concentrated on one major client (MSFT). While this deal is a "golden ticket" that will enable the company to realize all of its ambitious plans, the company is validating its business model by making itself dependent on a single counterparty. Any problems with MSFT will lead to a sharp correction in IREN's share price. One of the reasons for this could be the same delays in scaling the cloud infrastructure for AI computing. With the focus on a single customer, there is a risk of shareholder equity dilution, as in December 2025, together with the issue of convertible bonds, there was an additional issue of 39.7 million shares at a price of $41.12, allowing the company to raise an additional $1.63 billion in capital.

Conclusion

Overall, IREN's fundamental analysis shows that its shares are really attractive for investors. One of the main reasons for IREN's continued growth will be the successful transformation of its business model, which will see AI Cloud's share of revenue jump, becoming the main business area. Taking into account the expectations from the implementation of the contract with MSFT and the achievement of a margin of 85%, it is a realistic goal. Therefore, this will allow the company to reach a new level of competitive development, enabling it to grow in value by 88% based on its EV/Sales multiples.

data.title: CES 2026: Autonomous Driving Hits An Inflection Point
data.description:
Mobility once again emerged as a dominant theme at the 2026 Consumer Electronics Show (CES). More notably, for the first time since the early 2020s, the industry’s center of gravity shifted away from electric vehicles (EVs) and back toward autonomous driving. In our view, this transition is being enabled by meaningful advances in both hardware and software, driven by the rapid scaling of the broader AI ecosystem over the past few years. Nvidia’s Alpamayo physical AI platform - announced at CES - exemplifies this shift, aiming to accelerate autonomous driving development by dramatically expanding real-world and simulated data, a critical step toward higher levels of autonomy.

As such, we see a clear inflection point forming for the mobility sector. Robotaxi companies such as Waymo and Zoox noted plans to expand services into new markets in 2026, and Uber also expects to launch its autonomous ride service this year.1 Several traditional automakers also featured more advanced driver assist systems. Additionally, Caterpillar (CAT) reinforced autonomy in its keynote, showcasing next-generation autonomous heavy equipment for construction and mining that integrates AI, machine learning, and edge computing to improve productivity and safety. 2

Across the show floor, demonstrations featured autonomous vehicle platforms, advances in LiDAR and radar sensing, next-generation battery technologies, and increasingly sophisticated in-vehicle infotainment systems, underscoring how intelligence, automation, and connectivity are converging to reshape the future of mobility.

This is one of two pieces on the leading themes from CES 2026. Read the companion article, CES 2026: AI and Robotics Shift from Hype to Deployment.

Automakers Are Making Meaningful Progress Towards L4 Autonomy

Several automakers and suppliers highlighted progress towards level 4 (L4) autonomous driving systems, with a ramp-up to L3 and L4 systems becoming a reality. Most of the currently available autonomous systems are L2 or L2++, requiring the driver to monitor the system and take control when needed. L3 systems are where a sizeable leap to autonomy takes place. The vehicle is capable of driving itself in certain conditions and drivers no longer have to monitor the system constantly, although one is still needed in case an intervention request is made. L4 systems are designed to respond to unpredictable surroundings without the need for any human intervention, removing the need for a driver.

Our team noticed that the rideshare industry remains a clear leader in L4 autonomy. Waymo (WAYMO) featured Ojai, its minivan-like robotaxi that is built by Chinese automaker Geely (GELYF), which is expected to launch into commercial service by late 2026. The van operates autonomously using 13 cameras, six radar sensors, and four LiDAR sensors.3 Waymo has logged over 100 million miles of L4 autonomous driving, and the company is now working on expanding into more than a dozen cities over the next year, including cold weather climates like Denver and Indianapolis.4

Hyundai also highlighted its Ioniq 5 robotaxi, while Uber (UBER) featured its Gravity robotaxi in partnership with automaker Lucid (LCID) and autonomous driver platform provider Nuro. Lucid is scheduled to begin production later this year in Arizona, and Uber plans to begin commercial use of the robotaxi in San Francisco in late 2026. Privately-held Tensor also debuted what it is calling ‘the world’s first personal robocar.’ The car is fully designed with L4 autonomy in mind, including a retractable steering wheel. Orders are first being opened up to customers in the Middle East.

Waymo and Uber Highlighted Upcoming L4 Robotaxi Models

Sources: Global X ETFs with information derived from: Waymo. (2026, Jan 6). CES Presentation.; Lucid. (2026, Jan 8). CES Presentation.

Traditional automakers also highlighted progress towards higher autonomy. BMW (BMWKY) showcased its electric iX3 model with a L2 driver assist system that can provide steering assistance, speed control, and potentially enhanced safety. Ford (F) is rolling out an AI-powered voice assistant in late 2026, and the automaker plans to include L3 autonomy systems alongside a new universal electric vehicle platform by 2028.5

Automakers also remain focused on user experience as the industry begins to adopt L3 and L4 systems on a broader scale. With less or even no focus needed on the road, infotainment systems and personalization can become a central piece of the experience. This creates opportunities for fully digital, customizable dashboards, cabin intelligence systems, dynamic lighting, and personalized messages or advertisements that can be seen on the outside windowpanes, expanding the opportunity set for companies in the autonomy value chain.

Aumovio’s Window Projection Technology Could Enhance Safety, Personalization in Future Vehicles

Sources: Global X ETFs with information derived from: Aumovio. (2026, January 8). CES Presentation.

Partnerships Take Center Stage, Offering the Potential to Accelerate Autonomy and Electrification

Partnerships appear to be more pivotal than ever before on the path to fully autonomous vehicles. One of the most notable announcements was NVIDIA (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang unveiling the Alpamayo tech platform, which includes open-source vision language models alongside simulated and physical AI driving datasets.6 With the physical AI system, Nvidia aims to provide researchers and automakers a foundation for building ‘thinking’ autonomous systems. In addition, the open physical and simulated datasets can help automakers more quickly build systems that can handle different geographies and the ‘long-tail’ of potential driving scenarios.

Mercedes-Benz (MBGAF) and Lucid are among the companies already using the Alpamayo driving model. During our visit with Lucid, the automaker’s management team noted how the Nvidia partnership can help speed up the development of advanced driver assistance system (ADAS) functionality from L2++ to L3/L4. The partnership between Lucid, Uber, and Nuro has also likely contributed to all three companies scaling their autonomous capabilities. The three companies are initially planning to deploy 20,000 Lucid-Nuro robotaxis across U.S. and international markets, with the potential for additional orders over the coming years.7

Privately-held autonomous long-haul trucking company Aurora noted how its partnership with German-based tech manufacturer Aumovio (AMVOY) can help bring down hardware costs while producing at scale. Aumovio will also provide a backup computer in the event the primary system fails. The two companies are also working with Amazon Web Services (AWS) to further accelerate ADAS technology development while ensuring stringent safety standards.8

Original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) and suppliers remain committed to bringing down costs and further improving efficiency for EVs. For example, progress being made towards the commercialization of solid-state batteries stood out, with companies like Schaeffler (SFFLY) and Donut Labs featuring models for cars and motorcycles, respectively. Schaeffler is currently working with three OEMs and two cell manufacturers to test and scale its solid-state technology, aiming to bring it to production by 2030. Next-gen solid state batteries could offer significantly higher energy density, faster charging, and enhanced safety.9 Additionally, Sony Honda Mobility, a joint venture between Sony and Honda, unveiled its latest AFEELA 1 EV model, which is expected to roll out later this year.

Autonomous Applications Continue to Expand Beyond Passenger Vehicles

Deere (DE) featured its highly automated X9 combine harvester, which uses the company’s tech stack, cameras, and satellite imagery to monitor and identify crops. The self-driving machine can auto adjust speed, harvesting, and processing settings, boosting efficiency and minimizing crop losses. Deere estimates that the combine’s predictive ground speed automation can potentially boost productivity by 20%.10

Deere also displayed its Vogele Road Paver, extending its agriculture-grade technology stack into road construction to automate steering and road-width adjustments. The equipment could lead to significant material savings while addressing labor shortage and safety concerns.

In previous years, Deere featured its autonomous tractors and precision sprayers at CES. As of December 2025, the company has more than 1 million connected machines in operation, with its digital operations center platform covering more than 500 million engaged acres around the world.11

Deere Featured Its Semi-Autonomous Combine Harvester and Road Paver Equipment

Sources: Global X ETFs with information derived from: John Deere. (2026, Jan 7). CES Presentation.

Kubota (KUBTY) also showcased its AI-powered agriculture equipment, including a narrow tractor and ‘transformer’ robot that could be used for multiple applications. Kubota’s Digital Twinning System, which leverages data collected by the real-world equipment in the field, was also featured as a solution that can help boost precision farming and operational efficiency.

Caterpillar showcased semi-autonomous and autonomous machinery for construction and mining operations, which could boost site efficiency and safety. Aurora discussed their progress with L4 autonomous long-haul trucking in the United States, which could double truck utilization and help address labor shortages. The company launched its first commercial self-driving trucking service in Texas in May 2025.

Kubota Presented Next-Gen Equipment and Digital Twin Systems as the Future of Smart Agriculture

Sources: Global X ETFs with information derived from: Kubota. (2026, Jan 8). CES Presentation.

Conclusion: Mobility’s Advancements Deserve Market Attention

CES 2026 once again underscored the rapid acceleration towards a more autonomous and electric future for mobility. Robotaxis remain at the forefront of L4 autonomy. However, more strategic partnerships and the introduction of Nvidia’s Alpamayo model could accelerate the speed to market on additional advanced autonomous models. These technologies set a foundation for a future where passenger vehicles, trucks, and industrial equipment are smarter, safer, more efficient, and more dynamic.

  2. CES. (2026, January 7). CES 2026 Keynote with Caterpillar's Joe Creed [Video]. YouTube.
  3. Car and Driver. (2026, January 7). The Waymo Ojai Will Soon Offer Autonomous Rides Around the U.S.
  4. Waymo. (n.d.). Safety. Accessed on January 15, 2026.
  5. Car and Driver. (2026, January 8). Ford Will Debut Eyes-Off Autonomous Driving on Its Upcoming $30K EV.
  6. NVIDIA. (n.d.). NVIDIA Alpamayo. Accessed January 13, 2026.
  7. Nuro. (n.d.). Built by Lucid, Driven by Nuro, Available Exclusively on Uber. Accessed January 13, 2026.
  8. Aumovio. (2026, January 6). AUMOVIO and AWS join forces to transform autonomous driving development.
  9. Car and Driver. (2025, January 5). What Are Solid-State Batteries, and Why Do They Matter for Electric Vehicles?

Market Review

U.S. equities advanced during the fourth quarter, with the S&P 500 Index finishing higher as sector leadership shifted from Information Technology to Health Care, driven by a rebound in pharmaceutical stocks. Stabilizing inflation and interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve (Fed) made for a supportive macroeconomic environment, while continued investment in artificial intelligence (AI) boosted a diverse cross section of companies, including elements of the semiconductor supply chain and companies that stand to benefit from AI infrastructure build-out. Within the S&P 500, the Health Care, Communication Services and Financials sectors were the strongest performers. Conversely, the Real Estate, Utilities and Consumer Staples sectors were the weakest performers for the quarter as investors continued to disfavor defensive sectors.

Quarterly Performance Review

Financials sector holdings detracted

The Fund (Investor Shares) returned 1.59% (net of fees) during the quarter, underperforming the S&P 500 Index’s 2.66%. Our holdings in the Financials, Materials and Industrials sectors underperformed, while holdings in the Communication Services and Health Care sectors, and a lack of exposure to Utilities and Energy, boosted relative results.

Top Quarterly Contributors

Eli Lilly (LLY) rebounded sharply in the quarter as concerns around pricing, penetration and competitive dynamics for the pharmaceutical company’s GLP-1 weight-loss drugs eased following stronger-than-expected demand data.

Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO), a global provider of pharmaceutical research instruments and services, recovered after being oversold earlier in the year, aided by results and guidance that exceeded muted expectations and early signs of end-market recovery.

Danaher (DHR), a maker of diagnostics technology, benefited during the quarter from improving sentiment around life sciences end markets, as pharmaceutical customers signaled higher-than-expected spending on research and development.

Applied Materials (AMAT), which supplies semiconductor manufacturing equipment, outperformed as optimism around AI-driven semiconductor capital spending persisted, supported by improving prospects at key customers such as Intel.

Alphabet (GOOGL) posted strong performance in the quarter as resilient revenues for its search business, improving growth in its cloud segment and renewed confidence in its vertically integrated AI strategy lifted investor sentiment.

Top Annual Contributors

KLA (KLAC), which provides process control solutions for semiconductor manufacturing, gained during the year on the strength of its exposure to leading-edge AI chip manufacturing, benefiting from stronger-than-expected demand and capacity expansion.

Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), a manufacturer of processors for computing and gaming, advanced significantly over the year as skepticism around its data center graphics processing unit (GPU) business faded and major customer wins helped de-risk its competitive positioning.

Applied Materials benefited from sustained AI-driven semiconductor demand, with improving customer outlooks supporting the stock.

Apple (AAPL) underperformed the market as concerns over tariffs and the competitiveness of its AI capabilities weighed on shares, making the Fund’s underweight position a relative contributor; sentiment began to recover later in the period.

Alphabet transitioned over the year from being perceived as an AI risk to an AI winner, as growth in YouTube and in its search and cloud businesses accelerated and the performance of its large language models gained credibility with investors.

Portfolio Positioning

Positioning for uncertainty and leaning into technology adopters

While equities gained in both the fourth quarter and calendar-year 2025, both periods were marked by considerable periods of volatility. With continued risk of turbulence in the market, we have been tilting the portfolio toward a more defensive positioning while taking offensive positions in areas where our convictions are highest.

Within our offensive positioning, we have taken advantage of market volatility to lean into secular trends through investing in AI beneficiaries including hyperscalers, semiconductors and attractive areas of software, which we view as an “anti-bubble” strategy. The portfolio’s largest absolute sector weight continues to be Information Technology, although the portfolio ended the quarter underweight the benchmark.

We added one new position during the quarter, the home- goods retailer Home Depot, in the Consumer Discretionary sector. While the company has long been the dominant player in its space, the stock experienced a bout of underperformance in recent periods in a weak housing market. However, we viewed the entry price as fairly attractive in relation to what we consider durable secular trends around aging in place and an eventual housing recovery in the next year and a half.

We exited four stocks during the quarter. In Financials, we exited Fiserv after the company underwent a change in management and reset its earnings algorithm. In Health Care, we sold Vertex Pharmaceuticals, which issued some negative updates to its pain franchise during 2025. Our original thesis regarding credit bureau Equifax hinged on a mortgage volume recovery, which we opted to alternatively pursue through Home Depot, so we closed the position. We also decided to move on from Ball, a manufacturer of aluminum packaging. Although its end market is slowly recovering, its risk/reward profile is no longer attractive.

We continue to view AI as a generational demand driver, creating a durable need for faster, more powerful and energy-efficient computing. We are also leaning into “adopters” of technology that are poised for U.S. exceptionalism, such as Eli Lilly and Boston Scientific, that continue to innovate to improve patient outcomes. We favor life science tools companies such as Danaher and Thermo Fisher, which provide valuable equipment and services for clinical research. We have high conviction in Deere & Co., which is using precision agriculture to drive better outcomes for farmers. We also favor nonbank financials, such as information exchanges like S&P Global and Intercontinental Exchange, which are using AI to increase the value of their proprietary data. These companies are durable. They are innovating and they are using a massive R&D spend to lead their markets.

We remain disciplined about staying fully invested and focusing on high-quality companies that offer the potential for market upside and resilience in market downturns.

Outlook

Continued optimism with expected market broadening

We believe the S&P 500 continues to be an extraordinary asset class made up of companies that innovate, invest, adapt and deliver high returns on capital. U.S. companies are investing significantly more in their future than their global peers, with capital expenditures in AI infrastructure becoming even more pronounced in 2025.

As investors in AI, we continue to believe AI technology is a lasting innovation that will transform the global economy. We are likely in the early stages of a decade-long AI investment cycle, in which we are seeking upside capture while managing the risks of rapid technological change, rising competition and growing financial leverage. We believe the gap will widen over the next few years between AI winners versus AI losers, a dynamic that can increasingly favor active portfolio management over a passive strategy.

Looking ahead to 2026, we temper our optimism with the reality that stocks don’t rise forever at the fast rate we’ve seen in recent years. Investors should anticipate potential stock price corrections driven by short-term concerns over AI growth. The challenge—and opportunity—for investors is to separate the companies that are over-investing from those that will be able to compound value through multiple cycles.

More broadly, we have been experiencing a “K-shaped” economy in which the aggregate numbers look fine but the feeling of prosperity hasn’t been shared evenly. Still, what has mattered more for the markets is that the economy continues to grow modestly while fiscal stimulus and global liquidity remain supportive.

How does all of this inform the outlook for 2026? After three incredibly strong years, we expect the S&P 500 to be capable of delivering another year of double-digit returns. The earnings growth gap between the Mag 7 tech giants and the other 493 stocks in the S&P 500 appears to be closing. As that growth gap narrows, and as liquidity helps cyclicals recover, we expect the market to broaden. In this environment, active management becomes vital as rising stock dispersion and sector rotation reward fundamental stock selection and risk discipline.

Throughout any volatility, we will continue to execute our investment process and seek opportunities to invest in increasingly relevant, competitively advantaged businesses at attractive valuations to deliver strong long-term returns.

data.title: LendingTree: Insurance Momentum Remains Strong
data.description:
In my first coverage of LendingTree (TREE) 3 years ago, I rated the stock a buy, citing potential undervaluation at the time. I was proven right, since TREE would then trend upwards and surpass the lowest end of my target price range of $39 per share.

Fast forward to today, TREE is already trading at $68 per share, up by almost 1.7x from the then-current price of $25 per share. A lot of that overperformance seems to be driven by TREE's over 32% YTD surge, possibly due to the improving operating leverage as well as a favorable macro environment.

I rate TREE a buy. My 1-year target price of $78 per share projects a 15% upside from the current price of $68. TREE appears undervalued.

Financials Review

Overall, revenue growth was quite solid in Q3, with TREE delivering $307.8 million of quarterly revenue, an 18% YoY growth. At that level, TREE pretty much just maintained its consecutive quarter of YoY growth.

While all segments delivered double-digit growth, contributing to the overperformance, the primary growth driver appears to be the insurance business, which grew 20% YoY to over $200 million.

Profitability has also improved YoY. While in Q3 last year, TREE realized a -$58 million GAAP net loss; that figure saw a massive rebound to $10.2 million in Q3. Adjusted EBITDA, meanwhile, also saw a 48% YoY growth to $39.8 million in Q3.

TREE's liquidity and cash flow generation have been decent as of Q3 2025. While cash and short-term investments standing at $68.6 million at the end of the quarter was lower than that of last year, it was more due to the extinguishment of one of its convertible notes. TREE has mostly been cash flow positive from an operating standpoint. In Q3, operating cash flow (OCF) was $25.6 million. In the end, the biggest highlight in Q3 was probably the successful refinancing of its existing debt into a $475 million credit facility, which helps TREE lower interest expense and also extend its debt maturity.

Catalyst

Into 2026, I believe catalysts remain quite strong for TREE. In particular, I believe that the strength in the insurance marketplace could be maintained, driven by the uptrend in the carrier marketing spend to compete for market share in 2026.

As premium growth looks to stabilize upon 2024's massive premium increase, a 2026 outlook from Deloitte suggests that the slowing premium growth means that carriers are now turning their attention to focus on better customer acquisition, effectively benefiting marketplace channels such as TREE. This will be quite an important trend for TREE, since the insurance segment makes up over 66% of TREE's business as of Q3, essentially making it the core engine for the business.

Moreover, we see the increase in spending across the board, not just within the top 3 carriers, as per the management comment:

Importantly, we've begun to see a strong ramp in spend outside of our top three carriers, an important indicator of the health and duration of this cycle. Specifically in Q3, if you look at our 4 through 10 largest carriers in our network, so take out our top 3 carriers, those next 7 spend with us increased by nearly 60% compared to a year ago.

Source: Q3 earnings call.

On the other side of the business, the Home and Consumer segment, as per the management's comment in the Q3 earnings call, I view the potential of the Fed doing rate cuts into 2026 as a driver for mortgage rates trending lower to hit 5.75%, meaning that if the situation occurs, TREE could be seeing a surge in refinancing leads, driving revenue generation for the segment:

Like, I can't tell you at what exact point we trend to that level. But that -- when you hit that inflection point and it gets 5.75% and it keeps fading down below that, that snowball could build really fast, and revenue could blow up and VMD could blow up really fast there.

Source: Q3 earnings call.

Risk

I believe risk remains minimal to moderate for TREE. While the insurance industry seems to still be in spending mode with a relatively low likelihood of carriers pulling back on spending due to the drivers I covered earlier in the catalyst section, in my view, there is a good chance that any shift in the Fed's policy or inflation rebound may keep rates elevated. As such, this would neutralize the positive expectation of the mortgage refinancing catalyst.

Another area that is worth discussing as a risk factor in 2026 is TREE's traffic generation. In the earnings call, this was also covered by the management, who described Q3 as a challenging time for traffic generation from SEO due to the impact from the rise of AI-generated answers in Google. As a result, I believe TREE could see an increase in customer acquisition cost from more focus on paid traffic:

So SEO is one of those categories. It's been very -- I mean, I'll be honest, it's been very turbulent in Q3 as traffic has shifted and changed in legacy SEO. The entire financial services industry has been hit pretty hard by it. I would not want to be a company that's highly dependent on legacy SEO traffic. I'll make that statement. I think it's fair to say the era of ["free rein"] on Google is coming to an end. But then at the same time, the paid search traffic, which we are very, very good at ourselves is continuing to be very strong and growing. So we're happy with that.

Source: Q3 earnings call.

Valuation/Pricing

My target price for TREE is driven by the following assumptions for the bull vs. bear scenarios of the FY 2026 target price model, based on the consensus estimate:

  1. Bull scenario (60% probability) assumptions: - The Insurance business maintains its overperformance, driven by carriers' continued spending, while the Home segment sees revenue lift from the refinancing leads, driven by rate cuts. Forward P/S expands to 1.1x.

  2. Bear scenario (40% probability) assumptions - Forward P/S contracts to 0.6x as TREE sees SEO-related headwinds that force TREE to spend more on paid conversions as it is still figuring out the best traffic strategy. TREE lowers its profitability outlook.

Consolidating all the information above into my model, I arrived at an FY 2026 weighted 1-year target price of ~$78 per share, suggesting an upside of over 15% from the current price level of $68. I rate the stock a buy.

Conclusion

In my opinion, TREE's 2026 thesis is driven by the continued growth in its insurance segment, driven by the likely elevated level of carrier marketing spend into the year. There is a likelihood of mortgage refinancing as well if the Fed's rate cuts take place in 2026 to lower mortgage rates below 5.75%. While risk remains concentrated in the structural decline of SEO traffic due to the rise of AI search results, the overall impact could be mitigated. Considering the current price level and the outlook, TREE remains a decent buy today.

data.title: Why The BoC May Keep Rates Steady, Even As Inflation Ticks Higher
data.description:
Canadian inflation rose to 2.4% in December compared to the same period last year. Robert Both, Senior Macro Strategist at TD Securities, explains why this latest reading is unlikely to prompt the Bank of Canada to adjust interest rates.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: Canadian inflation edged higher in December, but will it be enough to have an impact on the Bank of Canada and interest rates? Joining us now to discuss is Robert Both, senior macro strategist with TD Securities.

Robert, great to have you back on the program.

Robert Both: Thanks, Greg. Great to be back. Helps to have a couple of days to dig oneself out after the storm.

Greg Bonnell: [LAUGHS] I know. It took me until yesterday until I felt physically recovered again. But I think it was a good workout. I think I might come out the other side of it looking stronger.

We got a fresh inflation report. I'm still getting used to the fact that they land on Monday mornings. What did we get in this report?

Robert Both: So, on surface level, this is the definition of a mixed report. The market expected headline or total inflation to come in around 2.2%. Came in a little bit hotter than that at 2.4%. We in the market were looking for core inflation coming around 2.7%. It came in at 2.6%.

So, on balance, you've got stronger headline, softer core, a bit of a mixed lean. But we think that there are very reasonable reasons that headline accelerated-- the biggest one being that we did have this temporary tax cut introduced last December. So that introduced a very large base effect. So, because prices pulled back last year, that means even without any change in December of this year, you did have an acceleration for things like food prices, bars and restaurants, children's clothing, some recreational components as well. So that is what drove the headline acceleration here.

If you look through that, the details were much more dovish. Those three-month rates of core inflation that the Bank of Canada has focused on in the past, those are now sitting below the midpoint of the Bank of Canada's target range. They're at their lowest level since 2024. So that speaks to these persistent underlying inflation pressures that have been holding core inflation back over the last year or two.

Those are slowly starting to fade away. And the core inflation backdrop is looking considerably tamer heading into that January BOC decision than it did three months ago in October.

Greg Bonnell: Let's talk about that decision that is coming next week. You get the inflation print. We are so consumed with inflation for such a long period of time, and for good reason. Did they put out their business outlook survey today? We got retail sales later in the week when we start getting all this information. What is the BOC thinking about the economy, thinking about interest rates?

Robert Both: So I think we start on the inflation side. That stronger headline, softer core that we saw today, it paints a pretty nice metaphor for how inflation has evolved since that last forecast we got from the Bank of Canada in October. So, their October monetary policy report looked for headline inflation to hold around 2% over the fourth quarter. We were at 2.2% in November. We actually accelerated to 2.4% in December.

So, headline inflation has been stronger than the bank thought. But those core inflation measures that are less impacted by temporary tax changes, volatile energy prices, and what have you, those have come in below the Bank of Canada's forecast. They were looking for those to average around 2.9%. We're coming in a little around 2.8%.

And again, when you look at the three-month rate of core inflation is an indication of where that momentum is trending. It's trending significantly lower than that. So, the last time that three-month rates of core inflation were near these levels was early 2024. We really only held there for a month. So if this recent deceleration is sustained, that would bring us back to levels that we haven't seen since late 2020, early 2021, before energy prices spiked with Russia's invasion into Ukraine.

So, inflation backdrop is looking considerably tamer. There is a little more uncertainty around near-term growth tracking. The Q4 data hasn't been quite as strong as the Bank of Canada looked for in their last forecast update. And really, the labor market has been that one bright spot for Canada. But we do think that you should be taking some of that strength with a few grains of salt.

Greg Bonnell: So when we take a look at all this, do we feel that this is-- we can take the bank at its word? They've basically told us they're on hold and props for a nice long period of time. We get a decision next week. I get the feeling that the house view is they're not going to do much next week.

Robert Both: We don't expect the bank to do much. We think they are going to stay on hold for the next year. But the Bank of Canada just acknowledged in December that-- or in the December minutes, I should specify-- that their next move might actually be a hike. That's something that markets have been pricing for the last couple of months. But the Central Bank always takes a little bit longer to put those words on paper.

Given the rapid deceleration in core inflation, given that weaker trajectory for Q4 GDP tracking, some of that enthusiasm around the timing of hikes might be a little bit unwarranted. What we're seeing from the recent data is that there is still a lot of uncertainty around the near-term growth outlook around US trade. That has continued to weigh on Canadian firms and households, and it's going to take a little bit longer for the growth outlook to stabilize.

So we do look for the economy to find its footing in the first quarter. We do look for Canada to start absorbing some of that excess supply that's built up over the last couple of years. But when we look at this morning's inflation data, we think the Bank of Canada can remain patient here. We think that the door isn't completely closed to rate cuts. But as they look forward at the timeline for bringing policy back to neutral-- because we are sitting below neutral right now-- they're certainly not going to be feeling in any rush to hike rates with core inflation coming off and activity on a softer footing.

Greg Bonnell: And for people who follow this as closely as we do, of course, longer term, you're thinking at some point you take away that accommodation, that would mean a hike in the key rate. I think when you talk to the average person on the Street, they've been hearing the story about cuts for so long, that it might surprise them a little-- what are you talking about a hike here?

Robert Both: It might be surprising. I think a lot of people are still getting used to the fact that rates might not be heading back to where they were between the financial crisis and the pandemic. But the Bank of Canada has been discussing this not just since rates peaked, but really throughout the last hiking cycle that the next five years might not look very much like the last five years. So it is going to take an adjustment.

That is one of the reasons that the household sector has been a source of uncertainty as well-- just all these mortgage resets. Households have been very reluctant to lock in at these levels for another five years. That adjustment to the psychology is something that's still going on.

Greg Bonnell: So we have, of course, a lot of uncertainty globally around trade relationships-- ours with the Americans, the Americans' with the Europeans. So, without the help of a crystal ball, what do we think about the Canadian economy going through this year? We got trade renegotiations or reviews coming up. I feel like it's hard to put your finger on any one thing here.

Robert Both: Yeah, so I think our view of the Canadian economy in 2026 is that trade and investment uncertainty is going to continue weighing on growth over the first half of the year. But we are also going-- we're not expecting large one-time shocks like we saw in the last year. When tariffs were imposed in March and April of 2025, export orders dried up. Some of those came back, some of those didn't. But without another rapid or sharp increase in US tariffs, we believe most of that bad news is already in the rearview.

As we look forward, we do expect to see a little more traction from fiscal stimulus. So, the federal government introduced about $60 billion in new spending between December 2024 and last year's budget. So that is going to continue generating more lift as we get into the next year.

We'll also be seeing a little bit more support come through from those last rate cuts. The Bank of Canada only brought policy into accommodative territory over the last three or four months, so we haven't seen the full impact of that yet either. And then as we do get into the second half of 2026, we do expect some of that trade uncertainty to dissipate just a little bit, and that should help unlock some more investment going forward as well.

data.title: Safe Pro Group: This AI Defense Stock Could Explode Or Collapse
data.description:
I covered Safe Pro Group (NASDAQ:SPAI) in August with a buy rating. The company, aiming to leverage AI for threat detection, saw its share prices decline 5.9% compared to the S&P 500’s 7% gain. That, however, only tells half of the story of the stock performance. The other half is that the stock rose sharply to $9.16 before reversing. At the top, the gain would have been nearly 35%. We also note that I had a $7.72 price target on the name, which indicated 17% upside. So, while the stock has been down since publication, I note that our price target has been reached and the buy thesis has worked out. In this report, I revisit Safe Pro Group and discuss why the name remains a buy with high risk but with some capital cushion after additional funding has been secured.

What Does Safe Pro Group Do?

Safe Pro Group is a company that provides intelligence services through drones and drones as a service for first responders. The company also provides ballistic protection and is aiming to leverage AI for threat detection. So, you could see the company as a play on AI and drones with an attempt to infuse AI in defense technology. The company benefits from patents on AI-driven threat detection and validation of its capabilities through the war in Ukraine.

The company operates through three segments:

  1. Safe Pro AI offers AI-driven threat detection and drone imagery analysis. This part of the business works with an AI-as-a-Service, or SaaS, model and is expected to be the company’s growth engine once commercialized and scaled.
  2. Airborne response works with drone-based aerial services for DFR solutions.
  3. Safe-Pro USA provides ballistic protective equipment.

While the rather obvious end market is military and law enforcement, their airborne response and Safe Pro AI solutions can also be used for the insurance and humanitarian end market.

The Risks for Safe Pro Group

The main risk for Safe Pro Group is that it currently has no product or service with scalable revenues. Additionally, the company is active in a field in which demand is not only high, but competition is intense. Safe Pro Group by no means is the only company that wants to capitalize on drones and AI-enabled solutions. So, fast commercialization is key. Investments from Ondas through a placement in August 2025 and a separate placement in October 2025 raised a total of more than $20 million. Using those proceeds to accelerate development of the AI-driven threat detection platform is about all de-risking the company can do, as it would address many risks ranging from scalability, revenue irregularity, unproven defense products, and lack of scale.

Profits Are Far Away for Safe Pro Group

During the third quarter, revenues declined substantially from $330,756 to $101,422. This was driven by Florida Power & Light, a major customer of Safe Pro Group, requiring less services. Work orders from FPL are a function of power grid stability and repair work, and that in turn is a function of weather. So, when the weather is good, there simply are less damages to the power grid, and fewer orders come in. The company is addressing the revenue variability through new revenue streams through nested flight services for FPL. While that is an extremely vague term, I believe it indicates that the company is exploring more regular flight services for FPL. Gross profit dropped by roughly $100,000 to $33,594, with margins dropping from 40% to 33%. Total operating expenses increased from $3.7 million to $4.2 million, leading to a $4.2 million loss. Adjusted for stock-based compensations, the loss widened from $1.14 million to $1.62 million.

In the first nine months of the year, the company burned through $3.5 million in cash from operations and $0.23 from investing activities. At the same time, the company raised $9.4 million from issuing stocks as well as warrants and option exercises and advances. This brought the cash position to $7.6 million. If that same cash burn rate is maintained, the pro forma cash position at the end of 2025 would be around $26.3 million, of which more than $20 million comes from investments, including $14 million from the investment of Ondas (ONDS).

Safe Pro Group is a High Risk Buy

The price targets and buy rating for Safe Pro Group heavily depend on the company’s ability to ramp up revenues. Analysts are expecting a substantial surge in revenues this year. So, the expectations for commercialization are extremely high with high revenue generation almost instantly, and that also provides a risk to the price target assessment. The company has a median EV/Sales of 28.9x, which would indicate 88% upside and an $11.51 price target. However, we do note that the EV/Sales ratio is substantially above that of the aerospace peer group. That is why I have also outlined price targets based on 2x and 3x the peer group, which provide price targets of $8.70 and $10.67, indicating 19% and 74% upside based on forward expectations for 2027. That is a wide range, but that is driven by how the market will assess the justified premium for SPAI once the company successfully ramps up revenues, as there are substantial growth opportunities in the years after. We do note that against current expectations for 2026, there is an 11%-31% downside, but I believe that is also driven by the market being forward-looking. The raise of capital also substantially derisks the need for further dilution in the near term.

Conclusion: Safe Pro Group is Looking More Attractive Now

Safe Pro Group remains a high-risk investment given that the upside hinges on an aggressive ramp-up of revenues. If the company manages, that provides substantial upside even if the EV/Sales ratio contracts, which I believe would be healthy. Investments from Ondas do strengthen the capital position of the company and can be seen as a sign of confidence in the company’s ability to ramp up its AI-enabled threat detection, which Ondas could leverage.

The downside is that if the company does not ramp up as quickly, we are left with a business that is substantially more expensive against its revenue growth trajectory and may also be less diversified. So, this year will be pivotal for Safe Pro Group to commercialize its AI-enabled threat detection platform.

data.title: Renewed Potential For Real Estate Investors In 2026
data.description:
By Bhavik Desai, Product Director

Resilient economic activity and easing financial conditions are setting a constructive stage for commercial real estate and real estate investment trusts (REITs) as the sector moves further away from pandemic disruptions and into normalization. With new supply muted across many property types and valuation gaps still evident, listed real estate enters 2026 with improving fundamentals and a more supportive backdrop for cash flow growth.

Economic Resilience Sets the Stage for REIT Recovery

Economic resilience and a measured path toward lower policy rates are helping to stabilize financing conditions and improve visibility for property cash flows. Independent sector outlooks point to balanced supply and demand and highlight areas of structural strength such as data infrastructure as well as steady trends in housing and logistics. This environment reduces oversupply risk and supports rent growth across many segments as development pipelines remain restrained.

Valuation Dislocations Present Opportunity

As seen in Exhibit 1, listed real estate continues to screen attractively relative to broad equities as represented by the S&P 500 Index. Earnings multiple comparisons show equity REITs trading several turns below the S&P 500. The recent strong performance of the US equity market relative to REITs, driven in large part by the outperformance of AI-linked tech stocks,[2] has resulted in equity valuation multiples soaring above similar REIT metrics. As of November 30, 2025, REITs are trading roughly five turns below their general equity counterparts. Since 2006, the average multiple premium has been less than one turn of the S&P 500 Index’s price-to-earnings (P/E) to equity REIT price-to-funds from operations (P/FFO). This ratio was significantly elevated during three periods: the global financial crisis, the COVID-19 pandemic, and most recently, AI-driven tech rally. These dislocations have traditionally presented buying opportunities for REIT investors. As cycles progress, these gaps tend to narrow, favoring listed real estate during periods of convergence.

Exhibit 1 - Earnings multiples for US REITs* and S&P 500 Index

On an asset value basis, REITs also look compelling, in our view. As of December 2, 2025 they are trading at a -2% discount to Net Asset Value (NAV), compared with a historical tendency to trade at a slight premium of about +2%. These discounts have historically reverted toward long-term averages as private market valuations adjust and transaction activity resumes. See Exhibit 2 for the share price premium and discount series.

Exhibit 2 - Share price premium/discount to GSA estimate of NAV

Unearthing REIT Sector Opportunities

Valuation dispersion across property types remains elevated. Several segments with durable structural tailwinds trade at discounts to estimated NAV, including Data Centers and Residential sub-sectors such as Apartments and Single Family Rentals. These areas benefit from cloud adoption, digital connectivity, demographic trends and housing undersupply. Self Storage REITs also trade at a discount, supported by steady demand and limited new supply. See Exhibit 3 for REIT sector premiums and discounts together with long-term averages.

Exhibit 3 - US Real estate securities valuations metrics by sector (%)

Health care stands as an outlier with a sizable premium to estimated NAV, reflecting strong investor demand and the influence of senior living valuations. While this premium suggests less near-term valuation upside, it underscores confidence in the sector’s fundamentals, which are supported by demographic tailwinds and low new supply. Office continues to register more pronounced discounts given lingering demand sensitivities.

A Positive Turn for Real Estate

After a period of softer growth from 2023 through 2025, expectations point to an acceleration in 2026, in our view. Consensus views anticipate FFO growth that is above the long-term average, supported by more constructive supply demand dynamics and improving transaction activity. Exhibit 4 shows a progression toward 6.5% FFO growth for 2026[1] relative to a long-term average near 5.4%. This suggests growth trends above historical norms, which would be consistent with a broader normalization in valuations.

Exhibit 4 - US REIT FFO growth (%)

At the sector level, cash flow growth expectations are positive across most sectors except Office. Health Care leads, supported by demographic tailwinds and limited new supply. Data Centers benefit from persistent demand for cloud computing and interconnection, while Industrial remains well positioned given tight vacancy and resilient tenant demand. See Exhibit 5 for cash flow growth across sectors.

Exhibit 5 - US real estate securities valuation metrics by sector (%)

The combination of dividend income and moderate cash flow growth underpins a potential improvement in total return profiles as valuation spreads compress.

A Positive Outlook for Listed Real Estate in 2026

As 2026 begins, we believe listed real estate offers a compelling combination of improving fundamentals, attractive valuations, and sector-specific opportunities. A supportive macro backdrop, restrained development pipelines, and accelerating growth expectations provide a favorable setting for active managers seeking to capitalize on valuation gaps and structural themes. While dispersion across property types remains, the overall outlook for REITs is constructive, with potential for both income and capital appreciation as markets normalize.

Important information

Source: All data sourced from Invesco Real Estate as of 12/15/2025 unless otherwise stated.

Footnotes

  1. Source: Invesco estimates based on Bloomberg and FactSet consensus data and FTSE Nareit All Equity REITs Index. Data as of October 2025.
  2. AI-linked tech stocks and their performance from 1/1/2025 through 11/30/2025 include: Amazon (Ticker AMZN) 6.30%, Nvidia (Ticker NVDA): 31.83%, Broadcom (Ticker AVGO): 74.57%, Microsoft (Ticker MSFT): 17.53%, Alphabet (Ticker GOOG): 68.42% and Meta Platforms (Ticker META): 10.93%.

FFO, or Funds From Operations, is a key financial metric for Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs) that measures operating performance by adjusting net income to better reflect cash flow, primarily by adding back real estate depreciation and amortization and removing gains/losses from property sales.

The FTSE Nareit All Equity REITs Index is a free-float adjusted, market capitalization-weighted index of U.S. equity REITs. An investment cannot be made into an index.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

The opinions referenced above are those of the author as of December 22, 2025. These comments should not be construed as recommendations, but as an illustration of broader themes. Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. They involve risks, uncertainties and assumptions; there can be no assurance that actual results will not differ materially from expectations. The opinions are based on current market conditions and are subject to change. They may differ from these of other Invesco investment professionals.

The mention of specific companies, industries, sectors, or issuers does not constitute a recommendation by Invesco Distributors, Inc.

Investments in real estate-related instruments may be affected by economic, legal, or environmental factors that affect property values, rents or occupancies of real estate. Real estate companies, including REITs or similar structures, tend to be small and mid-cap companies and their shares may be more volatile and less liquid.
