data.title: Euronet Worldwide: My Favorite Fintech Value Play
data.description:
Introduction

Euronet Worldwide (EEFT) is a US-based payment processing company with a strong presence in Europe. The company specializes in owning/leasing ATMs and has recently been pivoting into the digital payments space. Challenges during the pandemic hurt the company's earnings and shares. Today, Euronet has recovered with solid earnings growth this year and good growth projected for next year with a valuation that makes the company shares attractive for value investors.

Euronet's Diversified Revenue Stream—Slow but Growing

Euronet has three different divisions that generate meaningful revenue for the company. The electronic funds transfer (EFT) segment includes ATMs, whose demand has been under scrutiny. As societies move towards more cashless transactions, ATMs are becoming less used. Euronet has diversified this segment by pivoting to point-of-sale outsource services and integrated financial transaction software solutions.

The epay segment is a transaction network that partners with retailers to offer prepaid solutions (like gift cards), among other things. The money transfer division assists with currency conversion and cross-border payments. This division sees seasonal growth when Americans travel to Europe, as Euronet is a leading provider of converting dollars to euros (and vice versa).

While the epay division has had a couple of subpar quarters, most of the divisions are generating high single-digit revenue growth on a year-over-year basis. Overall, sales growth is currently 4.2% higher than in the same quarter of a year ago. While investors are concerned about the ATM division and its eventual shrinking, management has stated that they are prepared for the ATM division to go from around 20% of sales down to 7% by 2034.

Margin Improvements

Euronet is not a robust sales grower, but its modest sales growth has been met with incremental operating improvements that have increased both gross and operating profit margins. While the company saw a nearly 10% drop in gross margins from the pandemic, gross profit margin on a trailing twelve-month basis slowly edged up from 35% in early 2021 to 41% in the third quarter. Operating profit margins have grown from around 5% to 13.2% over the same period. Continued margin improvements are another reason for investors to be optimistic.

Projected Earnings Growth and Valuation

These improvements have allowed the company to gradually grow earnings. For the full year 2025, Euronet is expected to grow earnings by 13% compared to a year ago. Analysts are expecting another 13% growth in earnings for 2026. While management has not specifically guided for 2026, they did say on the Q3 earnings call that double-digit earnings growth in 2026 was a reasonable expectation.

What makes the investment in Euronet so compelling is the fact that despite consistent double-digit earnings growth, the company is trading at less than 7 times next year's earnings. This is extraordinarily cheap when compared to higher attention peers PayPal (PYPL) and Fiserv (FISV), who have lower earnings growth projections and just slightly above Western Union (WU) and Global Payments (GPN), who are experiencing significant revenue and earnings headwinds.

Cash Flow and Share Buybacks

Another factor boosting the bullish investment thesis for Euronet is free cash flow. While free cash flow fell on a trailing twelve-month basis in the third quarter, it is still at a healthy level of $334 million. Additionally, Euronet is trading at 10 times free cash flow, which is in line with its peers. A boost in free cash back to last year's levels will drive the free cash flow multiple well below peers and create another value-related variance that justifies a strong buy rating.

Euronet has taken its free cash flow and conducted consistent share buybacks, which is also bullish towards the company's share price. Since 2021, the company has reduced its share count by 8 million shares down to 45 million. While the acquisition of CoreCard is expected to have added shares, I believe that continued share buybacks will continue. Despite the volume of share buybacks, the company has also managed to grow its cash balance gradually, which is now over $2 billion and provides Euronet with flexibility to explore growth investments.

Risks to Euronet

There are a few risks to Euronet's earnings worth mentioning. First, the company's epay segment is strongly tied to consumer discretionary spending in Europe. Economic factors such as high inflation or high unemployment could curb spending and hurt earnings. Another risk to mention is cybersecurity. Due to the increasing digital presence of the company, they could be affected by an outage or data breach, which would also hurt earnings.

One other risk worth mentioning is currency fluctuations. Because of the nature of the business with currency conversion, Euronet can be hurt by rapid currency fluctuations, especially between the euro and the dollar. Fortunately, the last fluctuation a year ago was nominal, affecting earnings by 3 or 4 cents per share, and the euro's current strength to the dollar is actually working to benefit the company.

Conclusion

While value in the fintech space has been dominated by discussions around Fiserv and PayPal, Euronet has been flying under the radar. The company is trading at less than 7 times next year's earnings while generating meaningful free cash flow and double-digit earnings growth. I believe the company is one good earnings report and positive guidance away from a meaningful breakout.

data.title: SPYV: A Value Version Of The S&P 500 With A Large-Cap Tilt
data.description:
The State Street SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 Value ETF (SPYV) is among the options following the value investment theme. The fund keeps a large-cap tilt and a relatively balanced sector mix, avoiding oversized exposure to defensive sectors. Yet, valuation and volatility remain below broader equity indexes. This credits the fund as an alternative for investors seeking diversification in a market trading at high valuations, and that has been largely driven by growth stocks. This article will detail SPYV's portfolio and evaluate its valuation, performance, volatility, and tradability relative to the S&P 500 and other value ETFs.

ETF Description & Highlights

SPYV offers exposure to stocks with value characteristics within the S&P 500, tracking the S&P 500 Value Index. This index is designed to identify companies from the S&P 500 trading at an attractive valuation, based on metrics such as book value to price, earnings to price, and sales to price, with the selected stocks weighted according to their market capitalization.

As of January 2, 2026, SPYV had $32.4 billion under management, invested across 444 holdings, with an average market capitalization of $162.2 billion. The portfolio shows a clear tilt toward large- and mid-capitalization stocks, which together represent nearly 80% of assets, with large caps accounting for 48.0% and mid-caps for 32.1% of the fund. For comparison, in the parent index, the S&P 500, mid and large caps make up 54% of the fund, as the benchmark has a large exposure to mega caps. Although SPYV also follows a market cap-weighted structure, its allocation to mega caps is much smaller at 17.8%, driven by a stock selection with underweight exposure to big tech names, as detailed throughout the article.

SPYV's top ten holdings include Apple (6.9%), Amazon (3.89%), Exxon Mobil (1.97%), Walmart (1.89%), Tesla (1.69%), Costco (1.51%), Home Depot (1.35%), Bank of America (1.27%), Procter & Gamble (1.23%), and Chevron (1.14%). These positions represent roughly 23% of total assets, with a clear emphasis on consumer-related sectors, alongside holdings in energy and financial services. Still, the two largest positions are Apple and Amazon, tech companies that keep a significant consumer-oriented angle.

The following table includes a list of value-oriented ETFs that will serve as the peer group for SPYV in this analysis. The largest fund in the group is the Vanguard Value Index Fund ETF (VTV), which tracks the CRSP U.S. Large Cap Value Index using a market-cap-weighted framework. Next, the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD) and the Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Value ETF (SCHV) also follow a capitalization-weighted approach but have the broader Russell 1000 as an eligible universe. The Invesco S&P 500 Pure Value ETF (RPV) uses value screens and weights holdings based on value characteristics rather than size, while the Pacer US Cash Cows 100 ETF (COWZ) selects and weights companies based on free cash flow yields. Finally, the Invesco S&P 500 Revenue ETF (RWL) rounds out the group by weighting S&P 500 constituents based on revenue, thus favoring companies with large top-line businesses.

Despite its value approach, SPYV's largest sector is technology, which accounts for 18.8% of the portfolio. This is followed closely by financial services at 16.1% and healthcare at 12.9%. The next tier includes consumer discretionary (11.6%), industrials (10.7%), and consumer staples (9.3%), in a relatively diversified allocation, while energy (6.4%), utilities (4.3%), communication services (3.4%), real estate (3.3%), and basic materials (3.3%) complete the fund's sector mix. As a result, SPYV shows overweight positions in consumer staples (+4.4%), energy (+3.4%), and financial services (+3.3%) relative to the S&P 500. At the same time, the fund heavily underweights technology (-15.5%) and communication services (-7.5%), given its limited stance on more growth areas that have a large presence in the index.

Breaking down SPYV's core holdings across the equity market, we see that in technology, the allocation is relatively diversified with 48 holdings. Apple is by far the largest position with nearly a 7% weight. Software remains an important industry via stakes in Salesforce, Oracle, Adobe, and chip design software tools maker Synopsys. The semiconductor sleeve is not as large as in the S&P 500, but it is still relevant, with holdings in Intel, Qualcomm, Texas Instruments, and Analog Devices. The fund also owns IT services names like Accenture and IBM, as well as a smaller networking exposure through Cisco and Motorola.

Holdings in financial services are also diversified with 70 stocks. Core positions include global banks such as Bank of America, Wells Fargo, JPMorgan, and Citigroup, along with card network giants Visa and Mastercard. The fund also owns players in the brokerage, investment, and asset management segments via Blackstone, KKR, Charles Schwab, and Goldman Sachs, as well as a smaller sleeve in insurance with holdings in Aon and Arthur J. Gallagher (insurance brokerage), Allstate (property & casualty insurer), and MetLife (life insurer).

In health care, there is a greater concentration in large pharmaceuticals via Merck, Johnson & Johnson, AbbVie, and Pfizer, while a still significant allocation to life sciences is led by Thermo Fisher, Danaher, and IQVIA. Other relevant holdings in the sector include medical devices players like Abbott Laboratories, Medtronic, and Stryker, along with managed care company UnitedHealth and Vertex, Regeneron, and Gilead in biotechnology.

Beyond these core sectors, SPYV still has core holdings across the equity markets, such as Amazon, oil majors Exxon Mobil and Chevron, as well as defensive areas including retailers Walmart and Costco, utilities NextEra Energy and Southern, not to mention communication services providers AT&T and Verizon, rounding out a portfolio with a tilt to mature companies with well-established and stable operations.

SPYV's largest sector gap versus peers is in technology (+7.0%). This is largely driven by a very light stance by certain peers, particularly VTV (11.1%), RWL (11.8%), and RPV (2.5%). The next two significant gaps are the fund's underweight exposure to healthcare (-3.9%) and communication services (-3.1%), also reflecting peers' specific sector positioning, including COWZ's 21.7% in healthcare and 10.7% in communication services, along with RPV and RWL's nearly 19% allocation to healthcare. In summary, SPYV looks like a value fund that still keeps one meaningful foot in technology, while most peers lean into more traditional value and defensive areas of the market. This dispersion in sector allocation is illustrated in the sector correlations. SPYV maintains a high correlation with SCHV (0.91), IWD (0.80), and RWL (0.79), but its alignment is much weaker with COWZ and RPV, where sector correlation is in the neighborhood of 0.40.

Valuation Below the S&P 500

SPYV's 18.8x P/E represents roughly a 16% discount to the S&P 500's 22.4x. The valuation gap stems from two key factors. First, the fund's underweight allocation to technology naturally drives valuation down. In addition, there is a clear valuation discount across virtually all sectors. These are more significant in technology, consumer discretionary, healthcare, and communication services, where discounts are over 40%. Individual holdings contributing to the fund's cheaper valuation include Merck (11.9x P/E), Verizon (8.6x), and Bank of America (14.6x), along with a portfolio that holds no position in high-multiple names like NVIDIA and Palantir.

The fund lags the S&P 500 in growth. Its historical earnings growth of 3.5% is well below the S&P 500's 10.2%, and its sales growth of 6.5% also trails the benchmark's 8.0%. Of course, the fund's underweight exposure to technology is a key driver. But stock selection plays a role as well, driven by a value profile, with core holdings in mature companies that have shown slow growth over the past years, such as Accenture, Wells Fargo, and Chevron.

On profitability, SPYV's headline measures fall behind the S&P 500. The fund's EBITDA margin (27.9%) trails the benchmark's 35.4% by 7.5 percentage points, and the drag is tied to stock selection that favors companies with businesses in lower margin segments, such as Walmart (EBITDA margin 6.1%), Costco (4.7%), Exxon Mobil (18.9%), and UnitedHealth (6.7%). Meanwhile, if we look at ROE, SPYV's 35.9% also lags the benchmark's 45.6%, with much of this stemming from underweight exposure to high ROE levels seen in technology, given the absence of holdings like NVIDIA, AppLovin, and KLA Corp, along with overweight allocation in names like Exxon Mobil (ROE 11.4%), Chevron (7.3%), Walmart (23.7%), UnitedHealth (17.5%), and Thermo Fisher (13.1%). In sum, it is a value-oriented stock selection that results in a portfolio with weaker profitability, especially compared to the rich metrics seen in the S&P 500.

SPYV has the highest valuation in the peer group. The fund is especially above RPV (11.0x), due to its marginal exposure to technology, and COWZ (13.7x P/E) as well, given its heavy allocation to value sectors like energy (16.5%) and healthcare (21.7%). Overall, while valuations among peers vary, all share a more significant value tilt. This is highlighted by an average overweight exposure to energy, consumer staples, and healthcare, mature sectors that naturally pull aggregate P/E multiples down.

Performance in Line With Most Peers

With annualized returns of 14.6% (3-year) and 12.7% (5-year), SPYV has lagged the S&P 500. This has illustrated a consistent underperformance that has marked the fund throughout the past years, with the exception of 2022, when the fund held up much better than the broader market, declining only 12.9%, driven by its more defensive allocation profile. SPYV has delivered returns very much in line with other value funds, though, despite somewhat disappointing figures over the past two years, when the fund has seen performance at the lower end of the peer group.

As a value fund, SPYV has delivered moderate volatility with betas of 0.92 (3-year) and 0.86 (5-year), well below the S&P's 1.00. The fund's volatility has been marginally higher than most peers, but still much lower than RPV, given this fund's greater exposure to mid and small caps, where volatility is typically higher than average. Meanwhile, the fund's contained price moves during bullish environments, highlighted by its upside capture ratio in the 85-90 range, reflect a tendency to underperform during rallies, similar to other value peers. This helps explain why these value funds have lagged the S&P 500 over the past years, as the market has been in a risk-on mood most of the time.

SPYV trades a heavy volume of $190.7 million a day. While it is not as large as IWD and VTV, this helps the fund's bid-ask spread to remain tight at 0.01%. Its turnover of 30% is moderate, but above more passive funds such as SCHV (7%) and VTV (9%). SPYV's fees are among the lowest in the group, given its expense ratio of 0.04%, sitting far below pricier peers like COWZ (0.49%) and RWL (0.39%). The weak spot is its 1.77% yield, which is light relative to the peer average (1.97%), trailing higher-yield peers such as RPV (2.5%). On the portfolio performance, SPYV's tracking error in the 6% to 7% range is lower than most peers, while the fund's Sharpe ratio of 0.84 (3-year) and 0.68 (5-year) places it at the upper end in terms of risk-adjusted returns, even if it does not match RWL (1.01 and 0.79), the top-performing fund in the group.

Final Thoughts

SPYV is a value fund that keeps a large-cap identity due to its market cap structure, with holdings generally in mature businesses, while still maintaining a meaningful footprint in technology. As a result, it is no surprise that the fund's valuation is higher than its value peers, though clearly below the S&P 500. Meanwhile, with policy and macro headlines still noisy, driven by tariff rhetoric and geopolitical tensions, investor sentiment has become more cautious at this moment, despite an overall positive economic backdrop that has helped boost stocks to new highs recently.

That creates a mixed environment, where, from an opportunity-risk standpoint, the fund's strengths are straightforward, with lower valuation and volatility than the broader market, especially in a market that may be more vulnerable to pullbacks. The trade-off is SPYV's slower growth profile that should limit the upside if the market's long-term uptrend remains intact With that in mind, considering the market does deliver something close to double-digit earnings growth in 2026, it is reasonable to expect SPYV to see earnings growth of mid-single digits this year, just above the historical trend, plus a dividend yield of 1.77%, which could be translated into a mid-to-high-single digit total returns, if P/E multiple remains at current levels. That said, my view is that SPYV remains a reasonable holding as a part of a balanced U.S. equity sleeve. The potential main swing factor may come from a multiple expansion if investors start to reward value, rotating out from more growth-oriented areas of the market.

data.title: Artisan International Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Process

We seek to invest in companies, within our preferred themes, with sustainable growth characteristics at attractive valuations that do not fully reflect their long-term potential.

Themes

We identify long-term secular growth trends with the objective of investing in companies that have meaningful exposure to these trends. Our fundamental analysis focuses on those industry leaders with attractive growth and valuation characteristics that will be long-term beneficiaries of any structural change and/or trend.

Sustainable Growth

We apply a fundamental approach to identifying the long-term, sustainable growth characteristics of potential investments. We seek high-quality companies that typically have a sustainable competitive advantage, a superior business model and a high-quality management team.

Valuation

We use multiple valuation metrics to establish a target price range. We assess the relationship between our estimate of a company's sustainable growth prospects and its current valuation.

Team Overview

Our team approach combines the benefits of strong leadership with the creative ideas of a deep and highly experienced team of research analysts. We believe this approach allows us to leverage a broad set of perspectives into dynamic portfolios.

Portfolio Management

Mark L. Yockey, CFA

Portfolio Manager

Charles-Henri Hamker

Associate Portfolio Manager

Source: Artisan Partners/MSCI. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized. Class inception: Investor (28 December 1995); Advisor (1 April 2015); Institutional (1 July 1997). For the period prior to inception, each of Advisor Class and Institutional Class’s performance is the Investor Class’s return for that period (“Linked Performance”). Linked Performance has not been restated to reflect expenses of the Advisor or Institutional Class and each share’s respective returns during that period would be different if such expenses were reflected. 1 Performance represents the MSCI ACWI ex USA (Gross) Index from inception to 31 Dec 2000 and the MSCI ACWI ex USA (Net) Index from 1 Jan 2001 forward.

Past performance does not guarantee and is not a reliable indicator of future results. Investment returns and principal values will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than that shown. Call 800.344.1770 for current to most recent month-end performance.

Investing Environment

Global equity markets delivered mixed but generally positive results in Q4 2025, as investors balanced easing monetary policy against slowing economic growth and elevated geopolitical uncertainty. In the US, concerns surrounding a potential artificial intelligence (AI) bubble intensified, leading mega-cap technology stocks to slow and, in some cases, stagnate following outsized year-to-date gains. This moderation in leadership, combined with broader economic uncertainty, fueled a market rotation as investors sought less volatile opportunities across other segments of the market. Monetary policy developments also played a key role. The Federal Reserve implemented three rate cuts during the year and signaled the potential for additional easing in 2026. As expectations for a lower rate environment solidified, the US dollar weakened significantly, declining approximately 17% over the course of 2025 and providing a tailwind to international assets.

Outside the US, equity markets reflected similar crosscurrents. European stocks posted positive gains during the quarter, supported by easing financial conditions, though returns were tempered by slowing growth and lingering political uncertainty. Asian markets also advanced, as investors looked past uneven economic momentum and rising geopolitical risk. Emerging markets, by contrast, lagged during the quarter, pressured by shifting capital flows, currency movements and softer global demand.

Geopolitical tensions remained elevated throughout the quarter but had a negligible impact on market growth. While intermittent progress toward a potential peace framework in Ukraine offered moments of optimism, broader risks persisted. China’s increasingly assertive posture toward Japan and Taiwan, alongside renewed instability in Latin America, including rising tensions involving Venezuela, added to an already complex global backdrop as markets closed out the year.

Portfolio Activity

In 2025, our portfolio delivered strong double-digit returns, outperforming the primary and secondary benchmark indices, the MSCI EAFE and MSCI ACWI ex USA. Our disciplined stock selection and thoughtful sector positioning were key contributors to performance. Our conviction in the industrials sector paid off, driving the portfolio’s performance. Other positive sources of returns came from our holdings in information technology (IT) and consumer staples. Stock selection in materials and financials hurt performance on a relative basis, although absolute returns in both sectors were solid.

In Q4, the portfolio underperformed both the MSCI EAFE and MSCI All Country World ex USA Indices. Our stock selection in industrials was the leading cause of relative underperformance. However, stock selection in the utilities and IT sectors helped buoy portfolio performance relative to both benchmark indices.

Our quarterly performance was largely attributable to holdings in industrials, particularly our defense theme. Two of our top-performing holdings this year, South Korean defense companies Hanwha Aerospace and LIG Nex1, declined in October and November. Investor profit-taking and a broader near-term pullback in the defense sector weighed on the shares of both companies, moderating the respective triple and high double-digit YTD gains recorded this year for each stock.

Hanwha Aerospace, the largest defense company in South Korea with significant exposure to naval, land and aero-based systems, continues to expand its global presence in defense spending. Our conviction in the company remains high. Healthy cash flow and order backlog will likely lead to near-term growth. Additional ammunition and land-based-system deals continue to be struck with European governments as they invest to become independently secure outside of NATO security agreements. The Middle East has also emerged as an area of opportunity for Korean defense companies, with specific interest in Hanwha’s K9 self-propelled howitzer and Chunmoo rocket system. In Q4, Korean defense names secured $8.5 billion of domestic and export orders.

On a positive note, utilities was an area of strength during the quarter. National Grid (NGG) and SSE (SSEZY) both contributed positively, reflecting investor confidence in regulated asset bases, long-duration cash flows and accelerating investment tied to grid modernization and renewable energy infrastructure. SSE received a strong positive market reaction after announcing a 5-year, £33 billion investment plan focused on upgrading UK electricity networks and expanding its renewables footprint, reinforcing the defensive and growth characteristics of high-quality utilities amid economic uncertainty.

Sticking with our theme of electrification, LS Electric also contributed positively, benefiting from increased global spending on grid modernization and power transmission infrastructure. The manufacturer and distributor of electrical power equipment continued to be supported by strong demand tied to electrification trends and international infrastructure investment, which we believe underpin attractive long-term growth opportunities. The company added significant deals to its pipeline during the period, the most notable being a contract to supply high-voltage transformers worth 460 billion KRW ($312 million) to a renewable power plant that will supply a large data center being developed in the Southeastern US. LS Electric is also expected to benefit from Japan’s commitment to invest up to $25 billion in US infrastructure and grid modernization.

UBS Group (UBS) was a meaningful contributor to performance, despite the portfolio’s overall financials sector underperformance this quarter. The financial services company’s shares advanced to 17-year highs as investors responded positively to indications that Swiss regulators may adopt a more balanced approach to future capital requirements than initially proposed earlier in the year. A more constructive regulatory framework would allow the bank to maintain balance sheet flexibility while supporting capital returns, helping it to maintain its competitive position internationally. Operationally, UBS continues to execute well, with resilient net new money inflows across its core wealth management franchise and steady progress integrating Credit Suisse. We believe the combination of cost synergies, improving operating efficiency and disciplined capital management will help UBS deliver sustainable earnings growth over the near term, even amid a more uncertain macroeconomic environment in Europe.

Pivoting to the materials sector, the portfolio lagged benchmark performance. Air Liquide (AIQUF), one of our larger holdings in the portfolio, experienced downward market pressure. We trimmed our position over the last few months based on valuation yet view the company’s strategic positioning in decarbonization and ongoing contract wins supporting durable earnings potential.

Position Activity

This year, we benefited from our investment theses in three macroeconomic themes shaping the world: financials, electrification, and aerospace and defense (what we refer to as Fed). Our adjustments during the quarter were modest and primarily reflected valuation considerations and ongoing portfolio risk management, rather than a change in our long-term investment outlook on global trends in the Fed themes.

Beginning with electrification, one of our largest adds this quarter was in Samsung Electronics. The position provided exposure to AI infrastructure growth. Samsung has been a direct beneficiary of high demand for AI-related chips, including dynamic random access memory (DRAM) and high-bandwidth memory (HBM). Recently, Samsung Electronics’ (SSNLF) sixth-generation HBM semiconductor, HBM4, recorded the highest operating speed in Broadcom's technical tests, underscoring the company’s accelerated expansion of HBM market share. Samsung’s strong HBM4 performance results boosted bullish sentiment toward semiconductor manufacturers, helping drive chipmaker stocks higher in Q4.

We added to our position in Elia Group, an electricity transmission company operating in Belgium and Germany. Elia is one of the fastest growing transmission system operators in Europe, supported by a significant acceleration in capital investment across the German and Belgian electricity grids, which emphasizes management’s expectation for net income growth exceeding 20% annually over the next several years. In our view, the stock’s current valuation does not fully capture the strength of Elia’s growth and the earnings visibility provided by the company’s expanding regulated asset base, particularly relative to historical levels and comparable utility peers in the portfolio, such as National Grid and SSE. Ongoing policy support for energy security and grid reliability further strengthens the long-term investment case for transmission operators positioned at the center of Europe’s power networks.

In industrials, we increased our position in MNC Solutions and Contemporary Amperex Technology Co Limited (CATL). MNC provides mission-critical power and automation systems that support defense-related infrastructure and security applications, areas seeing sustained investment amid elevated geopolitical uncertainty. While the shares experienced near-term weakness, we believe the company’s exposure to long-duration defense spending and infrastructure modernization remains intact. The company has delivered solid revenue growth, which we believe will grow by ~20% over the next couple of years, supported by its specialized product mix and long-term contracts. In a macro environment characterized by heightened geopolitical risk and increased focus on national security, we view MNC’s specialized capabilities and defensive end-market exposure as supportive of its long-term earnings profile.

CATL, on the other hand, is a potential key long-term beneficiary of global electrification. As we mentioned in previous letters, the company is a leading manufacturer and distributor of battery products, particularly within electric vehicles and energy storage systems. While near-term sentiment toward the sector has been volatile amid pricing pressure and slower growth in certain EV markets, we believe CATL’s scale, technology leadership and cost advantages differentiate it from competitors. Recent market volatility offered an attractive opportunity to build exposure to a high-quality franchise aligned with long-duration electrification tailwinds.

Alternatively, in industrials, we exited our positions in BAE Systems (BAESY) and Mitsubishi Heavy Industries (MHVYF), following strong performances over the course of the year. We believe valuations for these holdings were fully reflected in their price, leading us to realize gains.

In consumer discretionary, we exited Amazon (AMZN) after the stock rebounded sharply and approached our assessment of fair value. While we remain constructive on Amazon’s long-term competitive positioning, we saw more attractive opportunities offering better risk-adjusted return potential elsewhere in the portfolio. We also exited our position in MercadoLibre (MELI), an online trading site for Latin American markets, after modest gains, driven by improving profitability and margin expansion. Given the stock’s appreciation, we chose to lock in gains and reallocate capital toward opportunities with more compelling valuations.

As we closed out the year, we began increasing our portfolio weighting in consumer staples from 3% to nearly 9%. While it is not listed as a key theme for the portfolio, we have identified several names in the sector that enhance portfolio balance, while also exhibiting high-quality characteristics, such as strong brands, disciplined capital allocation and pricing power. We believe these attributes are increasingly valuable in a more uncertain macroeconomic environment, as they provide resilient cash flows and help complement the portfolio’s more growth-oriented holdings.

One such name is Tesco (TSCDF), a UK-based multinational grocery and retail company operating a leading supermarket chain. Now one of our top 10 holdings in the portfolio, Tesco continues to benefit from its scale advantages and price leadership, which have allowed it to gain market share despite a competitive environment and a UK consumer backdrop that remains value conscious. At the same time, margin contributions from initiatives in retail media and continued share repurchases support earnings growth without requiring an improvement in consumer spending.

Outlook

Looking ahead, we believe the investment backdrop in Europe and international markets more broadly continues to offer a compelling opportunity set, particularly as government spending becomes an increasingly important driver of economic activity. Across much of Europe, governments remain the largest source of incremental demand, with public investment playing a meaningful role in GDP through defense, infrastructure, energy security and grid modernization. Our portfolio themes are closely aligned with these spending priorities, particularly in areas where capital allocation is visible, has long duration and is supported by policy objectives.

Persistently elevated geopolitical tensions have led us to evolve our portfolio positioning in order to maintain a balanced opportunity set. We have been reducing exposure to the defense theme after a year of strong performance and redeploying that capital into electrification-related investments, where we see durable demand driven by grid expansion, energy transition and rising electricity consumption. At the same time, we believe European financials are increasingly well positioned, supported by improved balance sheets, more disciplined capital allocation and a stabilizing macroeconomic environment, even amid ongoing geopolitical uncertainty in the region.

International markets remain an attractive source of value and opportunity relative to the US. In 2025, US equities lagged global peers, with the S&P 500® Index rising 17.8%, compared with a 32.4% gain for the MSCI All Country World ex US Index—the widest gap since 2009. Against this backdrop, we continue to seek international equities where valuations are more reasonable and where earnings growth is supported by structural investment trends rather than multiple expansion alone.
